name: Hertzian Tales
short_description: Anthony Dunne's founding critical-design text proposing electronic objects as aesthetic and critical provocations that make the invisible electromagnetic environment perceptible.
long_description: Hertzian Tales is Anthony Dunne's 1999 Royal College of Art doctoral work (later published by MIT Press) that helped found critical and speculative design. It argues that electronic products should engage the social, cultural, and aesthetic implications of technology rather than merely package it, and proposes a set of provocative objects, including the Faraday Chair, Electroclimates, and Thief of Affection, that render the otherwise invisible 'Hertzian space' of radio and electromagnetic fields into tangible, debatable experiences. As physical AI and interaction design, it matters because it reframes everyday electronic things as designed encounters with an unseen technical environment, a lineage that informs how speculative and ambient interactive objects are conceived today.
ai_note: These are critical-design objects and design proposals about electromagnetic space; no AI or machine learning is part of the work, and none is even envisioned in its concepts.

images.caption: Cover of Hertzian Tales: Electronic Products, Aesthetic Experience, and Critical Design by Anthony Dunne (MIT Press edition).
